sources.title: Map of the battlefield of Gettysburg. July 3rd, 1863 (Third Day's Battle — Positions of Troops)
sources.author: John B. Bachelder
sources.editionNote: Positions compiled by John B. Bachelder, 1876; new edition 1883 (LC Civil War Maps 2nd ed. no. 326). Drawn on the Warren survey base at 1:12,000; PD-traceable. Encodes position CLASSES (first/intermediate/evening/all day), not clock times — every clock attached to a Bachelder position is an inferred time on a documented position. Union regiment geometry best-sourced; Confederate at-the-wall positions are brigade-level and the most interpretation-laden (Hartwig, GNMP Seminar 12).
sources.note: Primary July 3 position source. Landcover features (stone wall, road fences, copse, fields) traced from the georeferenced sheet 2026-07-02 into data/landcover/landcover.json.

sources.title: Battle field of Gettysburg (surveyed 1868-69 under Bvt. Maj. Gen. G.K. Warren)
sources.author: G. K. Warren (survey); P. M. Blake (field revision 1873)
sources.editionNote: 1:12,000 reduction of the 200-ft-to-the-inch original (NARA RG 77:E 81). LOC verbatim: 'Positions of troops are not given.' Terrain/roads/fences/vegetation base layer only.
sources.note: Georeferencing base for the Bachelder sheets and the traced landcover.

sources.title: The Battle of Gettysburg
sources.author: Frank Aretas Haskell
sources.editionNote: Wisconsin History Commission Reprints No. 1 (1908); written as a letter weeks after the battle. Famously partisan — 72nd PA veterans disputed his Angle characterization. Union 1st Lt., aide to Gen. Gibbon, on Cemetery Ridge.

clockProfile.assessment: Two anchor statements, both re-verified from the full text 2026-07-10: watch check 'five minutes before one o'clock' immediately before the first gun (stated ~13:00 vs chain 13:07 -> +7) and cannonade end 'at three o'clock, almost precisely' (stated 15:00; corrected 15:07, inside CA-J3A-5's envelope; ED-1's arithmetic was built on his stated time). His 'Half past two o'clock, an hour and a half since the commencement' is internally consistent on his own watch. Envelope reflects one-source derivation. Partisanship (72nd PA) affects his facts, not his watch.

sources.title: Military Memoirs of a Confederate, ch. 18 (Gettysburg: Third Day)
sources.author: Edward Porter Alexander
sources.editionNote: 44-year retrospective; times self-reported from his own watch. Confederate colonel directing the assault artillery on Longstreet's front (not Lee's artillery chief — Pendleton held that post). PASS-3 NOTE (archive.org full text militarymemoirso00alex greppable): ch. 19 verbatim anchors re-confirmed ('It was just 1 P.M. by my watch when the signal guns were fired'; the 1:25/1:40 notes; 'doubtless 1.50 or later'). Assault-column structure p. 419-420: the nine Richardson howitzers intended to advance with the column (see or-27-2-garnett-bn); the column diagram misprints 'McGowan' for Pettigrew's (Marshall's) brigade - flagged; Wilcox 'ordered forward 20 minutes or more later, was much too late to be of any assistance whatever'; the noon Bliss-farm cannonade that wasted Hill's ammunition; Federal guns 166 at the cannonade's start, 220 engaged in all, vs 172 CSA. His Wilcox/Perry epilogue: 'It was at once both absurd and tragic ... Wilcox moved by his right flank and making a circuit regained our lines at the Peach Orchard. His loss in this charge was 204 killed and wounded. Perry's loss was about proportional, with some prisoners in addition.'

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement, re-verified from the full text 2026-07-10: 'It was just 1 P.M. by my watch when the signal guns were fired' (stated 13:00 vs chain 13:07 -> +7). His 1:25 and 1:40 note times correct to 13:32/13:47 (CA-J3A-2/3). His step-off statement 'doubtless 1.50 or later, but I did not look at my watch again' is self-hedged and excluded from anchor statements. Confidence capped by the 44-year retrospective distance regardless of arithmetic; envelope held wide.

sources.title: Notes on the Rebel Invasion of Maryland and Pennsylvania
sources.author: Michael Jacobs
sources.editionNote: The most contemporaneous source (1864). Civilian professor at Pennsylvania College, Gettysburg; habitually precise but a distant observer.

clockProfile.assessment: The chain's precision standard: CA-J3A-1 (13:07) is defined on his 'at seven minutes past 1 p. m.' reading, so his offset is 0 by construction. Cross-checks: July 2 artillery opening 'at twenty minutes past 4 P.M.' sits at the late edge of that event's envelope (tablets say 'about 4'); July 3 morning 'From 4 1/2 to 10 1/2 A.M.' agrees with the tablet 04:30/10:30 pair. Full text re-verified 2026-07-10. Caveat riding the source record: his 'When 2 1/2 p. m. came' step-off (14:30) is the strongest counter-reading to ED-1 and is carried as a conflict record on CA-J3A-6, not as an offset datum.

sources.title: Battle of Gettysburg, and the Charge of Pickett's Division (SHSP Vol. 32, pp. 183-195)
sources.author: Rawley W. Martin and John Holmes Smith
sources.editionNote: Martin (Lt. Col., 53rd VA, Armistead's brigade) pp. ~184-189; Smith (Capt., 11th VA, Kemper's) pp. ~189-195. Martin's letter dated Aug. 11, 1897 — 34-year recollection; twice misnames the Union position 'Seminary Ridge'. Page span verified against the archive.org scan 2026-07-02.

sources.title: Pettigrew's Charge at Gettysburg (SHSP Vol. 7, pp. 91-93)
sources.author: Birkett D. Fry
sources.editionNote: Fry commanded Archer's brigade, the guide of the whole assault. Down wounded before the end; misdates Heth's wounding. Page span verified against the archive.org scan 2026-07-02.

sources.title: From Manassas to Appomattox
sources.author: James Longstreet
sources.editionNote: The fifteen-thousand protest at pp. 386-387 is indirect discourse in this memoir; the direct-speech version is his earlier 1877 account — do not conflate. Assault organization at pp. 388-389; strength figures at p. 315.

sources.title: Pictorial History Thirteenth Regiment Vermont Volunteers
sources.author: Ralph Orson Sturtevant
sources.editionNote: Ignores the known Stannard-vs-Doubleday/Hancock credit dispute. Flank-attack passage pp. 304-305.

sources.title: Lieut.-Colonel Frederick Fuger, U.S. Army and his Descendants (eyewitness account of July 3, 1863)
sources.author: Frederick Fuger
sources.editionNote: Family-published manuscript (Winn & Hammond, Detroit, 1904). Written ~41 years after the event during Fuger's Medal of Honor pursuit — a contested, self-interested retelling (Hartwig: 'Fuger's embellishments... gained traction'). His hand-to-hand claim is directly disputed by Anthony McDermott (69th PA, 1891). Wound/death sequence for Cushing comes from this variant.

sources.title: Cushing's Battery at Gettysburg (Journal of the Military Service Institution, Vol. 41, p. 408)
sources.author: Frederick Fuger
sources.editionNote: JMSI Vol. 41 has no verified free-and-scriptable full text (archive.org run stops at Vol. 39); the final-canister/Armistead passage is verified verbatim via the NPS GNMP citation (hartwig-2012-nps). Same 41-44-year retrospective caveats as fuger-1904.

sources.title: Lieutenant Cushing (From the Fields of Gettysburg, NPS GNMP blog, June 21, 2012)
sources.author: D. Scott Hartwig
sources.editionNote: NPS staff historian. Quotes the JMSI 1907 Fuger passage directly and documents the conflicts between Fuger, Cowan's 1885 Bachelder letter, and Webb's official report. Contains no clock times at all (fetch-verified 2026-07-02).

sources.title: A Brief History of the 69th Regiment Pennsylvania Veteran Volunteers
sources.author: Anthony W. McDermott
sources.editionNote: Full text verified. The gold-standard company-level fragment for the Angle (Cos. I, A, F, D).

sources.title: History of the Nineteenth Regiment, Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry, 1861-1865 (ch. XXIX, pp. 234-237)
sources.author: Ernest Linden Waitt (compiler)
sources.editionNote: Compiled regimental history — 43-years-later literary polish over veterans' testimony; composite enlisted-ranks voice for the Cemetery Ridge bombardment.

sources.title: Official report on the 71st Pennsylvania at Gettysburg (Lt. Col. C. Kochersperger / Col. R. Penn Smith)
sources.author: R. Penn Smith
sources.editionNote: Accessed via the Stone Sentinels reproduction. The report names no companies.

sources.title: Col. Norman J. Hall's report (3rd Brigade, 2nd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 435-440
sources.author: Norman J. Hall
sources.editionNote: UPGRADED from the unverified stub (previous URL: https://collections.library.cornell.edu/moa_new/waro.html). Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 via the civilwar.com page-by-page OR transcription (id = 188622 + page; OCR noise). Dated July 17, 1863, Pleasant Valley Md. Keys: brigade of five regiments, ~190 miles marched before arriving July 2; 'A few rails were disposed in front of the line, to form a slight shelter'; July 2 42nd NY + 19th MA sent to Humphreys, Barksdale 'mortally wounded and two colors left on the ground within 20 yards of the line of the Seventh Michigan'; July 3 'At 1 o'clock the enemy opened with artillery' ~150 pieces, 'At 3 o'clock exactly the fire of the enemy slackened, and his first line of battle advanced from the woods', second line 'About 100 yards in rear', the by-the-flank column movement; 7th MI/20th MA 'open fire at about 200 yards', some regiments 'waiting even until but 50 paces'; his own brigade moved by the flank to Webb's support, 'rather giving way than advancing' ~10 min; 'Between 1,500 and 2,000 prisoners were captured at the point of attack', 'Twenty battle-flags were captured in a space of 100 yards square'; commanders lost: Revere (20th MA) mw, Thoman (59th NY) k, Steele (7th MI) k. PAGE GAP: p. 438 body text did not extract (only the OR editors' footnote '*Should be Arnold's', correcting Hall's naming of the battery on his right) - the Webb-support movement details on that page are carried from pp. 437/439; re-fetch route: ehistory serial 043/0438. PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 438-440 fetched 2026-07-11): the p. 438 'extraction gap' is EXPLAINED - p. 438 is a MAP PLATE ('MAP.') carrying the OR editors' footnote '*Should be Arnold's.' (the very footnote ED-40 adjudicates); no casualty table lives there. pp. 439-440 add the full melee narrative: the 7th Mich/20th Mass opening at ~200 yards, the ten-minute wavering line, the by-flank crowding, 'General Garnett and Armistead were picked up near this point' (CONFLICT: Garnett's body was never identified/recovered in the historical record - Hall's statement carried as a first-class conflicting reading, probably a misidentification in the aftermath), 'Twenty battle-flags were captured in a space of 100 yards square', 'Between 1,500 and 2,000 prisoners were captured at the point of attack', the flag-stealing complaint, and the regimental commander pins (Steele k, Revere mw, Thoman mw, Macy w, Wass w, Rice w).

clockProfile.assessment: Two anchor statements: opening 'At 1 o'clock' (chain 13:07 -> +7) and 'At 3 o'clock exactly the fire of the enemy slackened, and his first line of battle advanced' - conflating CA-J3A-5 (15:00) and CA-J3A-6 (15:05) at a stated 15:00, i.e. +0..+5. The 'exactly' is watch-adjacent language but the report never says a watch was checked. Rule 4: never moves anchors.

sources.title: Gettysburg Stone Sentinels (monument and unit pages)
sources.author: Steve Hawks
sources.editionNote: Monument-inscription and modern-compilation figures derived from GNMP tablets and OR data; the site's own disclaimer: 'often in disagreement between regimental and brigade records.' Two separate fetches of the same page have returned different numbers for some brigades (extraction caution). Strength figures are inferred-tier, not period documents.

clockProfile.assessment: The War Department tablets are ~35-year-retrospective adjudications, not witnesses. On Ewell's front they match modern scholarship well (Hoke 20:00; Johnson 'dusk'; retired 10:30). EXCEPTION (ED-28 skew rule): McLaws-wing tablets run systematically ~1 hour EARLY (Kershaw 'advanced about 4.30' vs modern ~17:30; Barksdale 'Advanced at 5 P.M.' vs modern ~18:15) - envelope [-60,0] on that wing, i.e. tablet-stated times there read up to an hour early. Site-extraction hygiene caveat rides the source record; the two 2026-07-10 re-fetches (Early 'about noon' = two miles out on the Harrisburg Road; XI Corps '10.30 A. M.' = Schurz's division specifically) are recorded in the verification dossier.

sources.title: Encyclopedia Virginia (Pickett's Charge entries)
sources.author: Virginia Humanities
sources.editionNote: Historiographic synthesis; frames scholarly consensus on assault strength as 'surprisingly hard to reach' (12,000-13,000 total; Pickett's division 5,800-6,200 depending on source).

sources.title: Col. W. R. Aylett's report on Armistead's brigade, July 12, 1863 (Museum of the Confederacy)
sources.author: William R. Aylett
sources.editionNote: Period after-action report, accessed via the Encyclopedia Virginia reproduction (secondary hop flagged). 1,191 total casualties for Armistead's brigade.

sources.title: U.S. Army Medal of Honor page for 1st Lt. Alonzo Cushing
sources.author: U.S. Army
sources.editionNote: Administrative commendation record compiled long after 1863, not a battlefield return. '126 men and six cannons.'

sources.title: Medal of Honor citation for Brig. Gen. Alexander S. Webb
sources.author: U.S. War Department

sources.author: National Park Service
sources.editionNote: Modern NPS synthesis: the two-serviceable-guns displacement to the wall, the three-wound sequence, and the 'fired the last shot' legend correction.

sources.title: Double Canister at Ten Yards: Captain Andrew Cowan at Gettysburg (Filson Club History Quarterly 59:3)
sources.author: Kent Masterson Brown
sources.editionNote: Read page-by-page in the 2026-07-02 survey. Cowan's arrival replacing Brown's B, the 5-guns-south/1-gun-north split (orientation corrected there), the 106th PA Cos. A/B skirmish detail (fn. 75).

sources.title: American Battlefield Trust timed map sheets, Pickett's Charge (3:00-3:30 / 3:30-3:45 / 3:45-4:15 PM)
sources.author: Steven Stanley / American Battlefield Trust
sources.editionNote: Copyrighted, look-don't-touch (ToS prohibits derivative/AI use of the maps themselves); brigade/time FACTS cited under Feist, geometry never traced. Brigade-level, its own clock frame — never silently mixed with witness clocks.

sources.title: Monumental Lie? — the 72nd Pennsylvania monument litigation
sources.author: HistoryNet
sources.editionNote: Survey-grade lead (SNIPPET tier) for the 72nd PA's contested position; used only to attest that the counter-reading exists, not for geometry.

sources.title: Col. Wheelock G. Veazey's report (16th Vermont), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 1041-1042 (appendix)
sources.author: Wheelock G. Veazey
sources.editionNote: UPGRADED 2026-07-10: full text fetch-verified via the civilwar.com OR transcription - the report sits in the appendix ('Documents received too late for proper sequence'), pp. 1041-1042, dated Camp at Berlin, Md., July 17, 1863 (NOT among the brigade reports at pp. 348-354; No. 71 there is Wainwright). Key verbatim: July 2 ~5 p.m. advance in support of the batteries, whole regiment on picket after dark; July 3 'AT A QUARTER BEFORE 4 O'CLOCK, THE ENEMY ENGAGED THE LEFT OF THE PICKET LINE'; 'AT ABOUT 2 P. M. THE ENEMY AGAIN OPENED WITH THEIR ARTILLERY SEVERELY'; 'AT ABOUT 4 P. M. THE ENEMY ADVANCED A LINE OF BATTLE'; the 16th passing 'BACK AND ALONG BY THE REAR OF THE FOURTEENTH' to the 13th's left for the first flank charge; the ordered march back and 'I RECEIVED PERMISSION TO CHARGE' against the second line; colors 'THE SECOND FLORIDA AND THE EIGHTH VIRGINIA'; Lt. Lawton mortally wounded 'NEAR THE CLOSE OF THE BATTLE'.

clockProfile.assessment: Two anchor statements: cannonade resumption 'AT ABOUT 2 P. M.' (vs chain 13:07 -> raw -53) and the infantry advance 'AT ABOUT 4 P. M.' (vs chain step-off 15:05 -> raw -55, or -65 vs the assault reaching his front ~15:15). A consistent ~1-h-late nominal clock, later even than Stannard's on the same events. Rule 4: never anchor-defining; his sequence statements (picket engaged 03:45, the two flank charges) are the evidentiary content.

sources.title: 13th Vermont monument inscription, Gettysburg
sources.author: Gettysburg Battlefield Memorial Association / State of Vermont
sources.editionNote: Inscription verbatim via Stone Sentinels: 'changed front forward on first company, advanced 200 yards, attacking the Confederate right flank... capturing 243 prisoners.' Monument text is veteran-committee testimony, Bachelder-era placement discipline.

sources.title: Report of Maj. Charles S. Peyton, Nineteenth Virginia Infantry (Garnett's brigade), OR ser. 1 vol. 27 pt. 2 pp. 385-387
sources.author: Charles S. Peyton
sources.note: Written July 9, 1863; the primary Confederate account of the road fences, the grass slope, and the breast-high wall on Garnett's front. Public domain.
sources.editionNote: Page-verified in full 2026-07-11 via the civilwar.com part-ii route (pp. 385-387; No. 441), superseding the NPS reproduction access (that URL kept as alternate). Written July 9, 1863, CAMP NEAR WILLIAMSPORT. Keys added at this pass: brigade 'reached the field about 9 a.m.' July 3 'in high spirits'; 'At about 12 m. ... position behind the crest of the hill on which the artillery, under Colonel Alexander, was planted'; cannonade 'opened at 1.30 p.m., and was kept up without intermission for ONE HOUR' (a primary short-duration statement - the ammunition-arithmetic school's kind of evidence, carried on CA-J3A-1/4/5 as a duration conflict); BOMBARDMENT APPORTIONMENT: 'During the shelling, we lost about 20 killed and wounded', officer pin Lt. Col. John T. Ellis (19th Va) k; step-off order chain: 'At 2.30 p.m. ... the order to advance was given, first by Major-General Pickett in person, and repeated by General Garnett'; 800-1,000 yards to the line; THREE post-and-rail fences climbed; the concealed first line in the grass ~100 yards before the wall; the mountain battery 'about 1 mile to our right, which enfiladed nearly our entire line with fearful effect, sometimes as many as 10 men being killed and wounded by the bursting of a single shell' (the Rittenhouse enfilade from the receiving side); the 75-paces relation with Kemper 50-60 yards behind-right and Armistead in rear; Kemper 'not being present [perhaps wounded at the time]' when his line lapped; the recoil at ~20 paces then the three-line rush; 'more than half having already fallen'; ~300 came off, every regimental commander k or w; ENGAGED-STRENGTH PRIMARY: 'The brigade went into action with 1,287 men and about 140 officers, as shown by the report of the previous evening' (~1,427 total; ED-46 candidate vs the ED-9/stone-sentinels 1,480); loss 941 with 'the majority (those reported missing) are either killed or wounded'; Garnett 'shot from his horse while near the center of the brigade, within about 25 paces of the stone wall'; the Spessard incident. Public domain.

clockProfile.assessment: Two stated clocks that do not move together: cannonade opens '1.30 p.m.' (canonical 13:07, offset -23) but the advance order comes 'At 2.30 p.m.' (canonical 15:05, offset +35) - internally compressed by his 'one hour' cannonade-duration statement rather than a uniform skew. Envelope spans both readings; the duration statement is carried as first-class conflict evidence on the chain (short-cannonade school), not as a clock. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: OpenStreetMap building footprints, Gettysburg NMP (Codori barn way 779833686)
sources.note: Positions measured into battlefield-local meters in docs/research/2026-06-13-landmark-anchors.md (barn anchor 4020.4, 4635.4). Facts/coordinates only; ODbL geometry is not redistributed as an asset. The mapped barn is the 1882 replacement on the same farmyard.

sources.note: Public domain; quoted here via priest-ecwc and gettysburg-network-1863 (no free full text located).

Antietam on the Web: 3-inch Ordnance Rifle
Official Records Series I Vol. 27, order-of-battle returns (Part 1 Union pp. ~155-168; Part 2 Confederate pp. ~283-291)

sources.editionNote: The OOB register's structural authority. Part 2 (Confederate) scriptable via the Texas ARK; Part 1 (Union) still has no verified script-friendly URL (standing open item) — Union structure verified via the OR-derived Wikipedia OOB articles at this pass.

sources.title: Battle of Gettysburg order of battle: Union (Wikipedia)
sources.author: Wikipedia editors
sources.editionNote: OR-derived compilation with per-regiment commanders and casualty annotations; wikitext fetched 2026-07-10 and used as the register's Union verification corpus. Secondary; the 2026-06-13 OOB pass confirmed it does NOT cite Busey & Martin page-level figures.

sources.title: Battle of Gettysburg order of battle: Confederate (Wikipedia)
sources.author: Wikipedia editors
sources.editionNote: OR-derived compilation; wikitext fetched 2026-07-10 and used as the register's Confederate verification corpus. Same caveats as wikipedia-oob-union.

sources.title: Regimental Strengths and Losses at Gettysburg (4th ed.)
sources.author: John W. Busey and David G. Martin
sources.editionNote: The standard modern strength reconstruction. NOT freely available online; no direct page-level citation has been obtainable in any research pass (2026-06-13 finding stands). Figures attributed to it in the corpus are via verified secondary reproductions only — flag the hop whenever cited.

sources.title: Lt. Gen. James Longstreet's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS reproduction)
sources.author: James Longstreet
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10. Report-nominal clock: gives 'About 2 p.m.' for the July 3 signal guns (~53 min late vs the proposed chain) and 'about 4 p.m.' for McLaws in position July 2. Never anchor-defining (ED-25 rule 4).

clockProfile.assessment: 'About 2 p.m. ... The signal guns were fired' vs chain 13:07 -> raw offset -53 min. Per ED-25 rule 4 this NEVER corrects into the chain; the profile records the raw skew so provenance UI can show 'Longstreet's report clock runs ~50 min late against the chain', and his July 2 'about 4 p.m. (in position)' is read with the same caution. Smyth's report shares the ~14:00 cannonade start (corpus pass-1) - a possible 'camp clock' cluster, recorded in the conflict record rather than silently corrected.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Harry T. Hays's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS reproduction)
sources.author: Harry T. Hays
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10. 'A little before 8 p.m. I was ordered to advance with my own and Hoke's brigade' — the best single clock for the July 2 East Cemetery Hill assault (CA-J2E-3); withdrawal complete 'about 10 o'clock.'

clockProfile.assessment: 'A little before 8 p.m. I was ordered to advance' agrees with its phase chain (CA-J2E-3 adopted 19:45-20:00, provisional ED-29): offset ~0. Kind still caps his claims at tier C (rule 4) - an agreeing report clock is corroboration, not an anchor definition.

sources.title: Maj. Gen. Henry Heth's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS reproduction)
sources.author: Henry Heth
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10. Division ordered forward 5 A.M. July 1; 'at this time-9 o'clock ... I was ignorant what force was at or near Gettysburg.' No clock for the attack itself; his July 1 narrative is noted in the literature as error-prone — sequence evidence, weak clock.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Ambrose R. Wright's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS reproduction)
sources.author: Ambrose R. Wright
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10. Gives 'about 5 p.m.' for the July 2 general-advance signal — ~1.5-2 h early against the modern clock for his own breakthrough; notoriously self-serving on depth of penetration. Report-nominal exemplar.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. John Buford's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 (secondary reproduction)
sources.author: John Buford
sources.editionNote: Report quote verified against a blog reproduction 2026-07-10 ('between 8 and 9 a.m., reports came in ... that the enemy was coming down from toward Cashtown in force'). His timestamped dispatch to Meade, 'Gettysburg, July 1, 1863-10.10 a.m.' ('The enemy's force (A. P. Hill's) are advancing on me at this point, and driving my pickets and skirmishers very rapidly... General Reynolds is advancing, and is within 3 miles of this point'), is VERIFIED as printed in OR 27/1 p. 924 (ehistory reproduction via Wayback; gdg.org transcription concurs; Coddington p. 682 endnote concurs) - a document pin at +/-0, the July 1 morning's hardest clock (CA-J1M-3). See docs/reconstruction/audit/verification-2026-07-10.md.

sources.title: Maj. Gen. Winfield S. Hancock's reports and 5:25 p.m. dispatch, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 366-378 (ehistory reproduction)
sources.author: Winfield S. Hancock
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10 via Wayback captures of the ehistory pages (ehistory blocks direct automated access; OCR caveat recorded - 'cannot will be taken' for 'cannot well be taken'). July 1: 'At 3 p. m. I arrived at Gettysburg and assumed the command' (p. 368) vs his own timestamped dispatch '5.25 [P. M., JULY 1, 1863.]' saying 'When I arrived here an hour since...' and 'we can fight here, as the ground appears not unfavorable' (p. 366; the P.M./date are OR editors' brackets) - both poles of the arrival controversy (CA-J1P-7/8). July 3: cannonade opened 'About 1 o'clock, apparently by a given signal'; 'After an hour and forty-five minutes, the fire of the enemy became less furious, and immediately their infantry was seen in the woods beyond the Emmitsburg road, preparing for the assault' (pp. 372-373); repulse p. 374. PASS-4 UPGRADE (pp. 374-375 fetched via the civilwar.com part-i route 2026-07-11): Webb disposition ('This brigade was disposed in two lines. Two regiments ... the Sixty-ninth and Seventy-first ... were behind a low stone wall'); the penetration ('When the enemy's line had nearly reached the stone wall, led by General Armistead, the most of that part of Webb's brigade posted here abandoned their position'); THE STANNARD DIRECTION CLAIM: 'I directed Colonel [General] Stannard to send two regiments of his Vermont Brigade, First Corps, to a point which would strike the enemy on the right flank' (the Hancock pole of the who-ordered-the-flank-move controversy; Vermont pole in benedict-1888/or-27-1-stannard); the second advance: 'A single line of battle ... numbering about 3,000 men, advanced, but it was utterly broken by the fire of the batteries on my left before it arrived within musketry range' with Kinzie's K/5th US and Fitzhugh's battery arriving 'just in time' (ED-41 corroboration); THE WOUNDING SEQUENCE PIN [D]: 'Toward the close of the main contest, I had the misfortune to lose the valuable services of ... Brigadier General John Gibbon ... severely wounded. A short time afterward I was myself wounded, but was enabled to remain on the field until the action was entirely over, when I transferred the command to Brigadier-General Caldwell' - Gibbon w BEFORE Hancock w, both toward the close; regimental commanders k list (O'Kane, Thoman, Roberts [July 2], Revere, Steele).

clockProfile.assessment: His July 3 'About 1 o'clock' at the signal (chain 13:07) puts his report clock within ~7 min of the chain, and his 'hour and forty-five minutes' to the slackening lands ~14:45-15:00 corrected - corroborating CA-J3A-4/5. Report-nominal kind still applies (rule 4): his clocks corroborate, never define. The dispatch timestamp (17:25) is a document pin, outside this profile.

sources.title: Maj. Gen. Oliver O. Howard's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 696-712 (ehistory reproduction)
sources.author: Oliver O. Howard
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10 via Wayback captures. July 1 ladder: orders 3:30 a.m.; march order 8 a.m.; Reynolds's death 'about 11.30 a. m.' with assumption of command; 'Schurz joined me before 12 m.'; 'About 4 p. m.' fall-back word to Doubleday; 'At 4.10 p. m.' positive fall-back order; 'At 4.30 p. m. the columns reached Cemetery Hill... General Hancock came to me about this time' (pp. 701-704). Party to the Hancock-arrival feud - partisan-dispute note rides CA-J1P-7.

clockProfile.assessment: No statement about a ruled anchor exists yet (the July 1 chains are provisional, ED-26/27), so the kind-default envelope stands. His 'About 4 P. M.' agrees with the XI Corps tablet verbatim; his 11:30 command time conflicts with Pfanz's 10:30 (open item, ED-26).

sources.title: Brig. Gen. George S. Greene's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 855-860 (ehistory reproduction)
sources.author: George S. Greene
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10 via Wayback captures. July 2: 'By 12 o'clock we had a good cover for the men'; 'we were attacked on the whole of our front by a large force a few minutes before 7 p. m.' (p. 856); 'About 10 o'clock... General Kane, with his brigade, was returning' (p. 857). Primary Union clock for CA-J2E-2's 19:00 assault start.

clockProfile.assessment: 'A few minutes before 7 p. m.' agrees with CA-J2E-2's adopted ~19:00 start (provisional ED-29) and with the Johnson tablet's 'dusk': offset ~0, envelope narrowed below the kind default on the strength of the tablet/modern agreement. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Lt. Col. Freeman McGilvery's report, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 881-884 (ehistory reproduction)
sources.author: Freeman McGilvery
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10 in two passes: pp. 881/884 via Wayback captures (verification pass), then pp. 882-883 READ AT LIVE EHISTORY in the browser pane (the earlier 'no capture' gap is CLOSED). Full-report keys: July 2 (p. 881-883): ordered to Sickles 'at about 3.30 p. m.'; the Wheatfield Rd line; the retire-by-stages fight ('At about a quarter to 6 o'clock... I ordered them to retire 250 yards and renew their fire'); Bigelow ordered to 'hold his position as long as possible at all hazards'; four guns left, recovered 'at 8 p. m.'. July 3 (pp. 883-884): 'early on the morning of July 3 brought them into line on the low ground on our left center'; the line roster VERBATIM ending 'total, thirty-nine guns' (the 39-gun figure is now PRIMARY, not secondary) including 'a New Jersey battery' and 'one section New York [Pennsylvania] Artillery, Lieutenant Rock [Captain Rank]' (OR editors' brackets - conflicts with the OOB's Rank-at-East-Cavalry-Field placement; ED-37 candidate); 'a slight earth work thrown up'; hold-fire orders 'not return the fire until ordered'; opening 'At about 12.30 o'clock the enemy opened a terrific fire upon our lines with at least one hundred and forty guns... most of the projectiles passing from 20 to 100 feet over our lines'; 'About one-half hour after the commencement, some general commanding the infantry line ordered three of the batteries to return the fire. After the discharge of a few rounds, I ordered the fire to cease'; enemy 'fired about one hour and a half, and expended at least 10,000 rounds'; his deliberate reply battery-by-battery; 'At about 3 p. m. a line of battle of about 3,000 or 4,000 men appeared... completely broken up and scattered by our fire'; the oblique raking fire through 'three extended lines'. Strength footnote p. 884: brigade took into action 15 officers + 368 men (organic four batteries). His '35,000' assault estimate remains a known overstatement.

clockProfile.assessment: Three anchor statements now that pp. 882-883 are read: opening 'At about 12.30 o'clock' (+37 vs 13:07 - his one EARLY clock), duration 'about one hour and a half' to his reply (consistent with CA-J3A-4's envelope as a duration), and 'at about 3 p. m.' for the advancing lines (+5 vs the 15:05 frame). Median +5; the +37 opening outlier keeps the envelope wide. His '35,000 men' strength claim is not clock evidence. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. George J. Stannard's report (2nd Vermont Brigade), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 348-351 (No. 69)
sources.author: George J. Stannard
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10 via the civilwar.com page-by-page OR transcription (Serial 43; URL pattern id=188622+page). OCR carries visible slips (quoted as printed, flagged inline in the dossier). Dated Gettysburg July 4, 1863. Key verbatim: arrival 'on the evening of July 1'; 'I selected a position to occupy... some distance in front of the main line'; 'The front thus established was held by my brigade for twenty-six hours'; 'At about 4 o'clock on the morning of the 3d, the enemy commenced a vigorous artillery attack'; 'At about 2 p. m. the enemy again commenced a vigorous attack'; 'After subjecting us for one and one-half hours to the severest cannonade of the whole battle, from one hundred guns or more'; 'the Thirteenth changed front forward on first company; the Sixteenth, after deploying, performed the same, and formed on the left of the Thirteenth, at right angles to the main line'; 'There were 350 killed, wounded, and missing from my three regiments engaged'. Wayback route to ehistory (used in the 2026-07-10 verification pass) was NOT reachable from this session's fetcher - access-route note recorded honestly.

clockProfile.assessment: Two anchor statements: cannonade start 'At about 2 p. m.' (stated 14:00 vs chain 13:07 -> raw -53; joins the Longstreet/Smyth ~14:00 'camp clock' cluster already flagged on or-27-2-longstreet) and duration 'one and one-half hours' to the infantry attack (agrees with McGilvery's 1.5 h and the CA-J3A-4 envelope as a duration, whatever the absolute clock). Rule 4: never anchor-defining.

sources.title: Col. Francis V. Randall's report (13th Vermont), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 351-353 (No. 70)
sources.author: Francis V. Randall
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-10 (civilwar.com OR transcription, OCR slips flagged: 'mu regiment', 'mew danger', 'Cantreville'). Dated Camp near Middletown, Md., July 10, 1863. Key verbatim: arrival 'on July 1, at about 5 p. m.'; the July 2 charge to the Rogers House ('we continued our pursuit of the guns... These guns, as I am told, belong to the Fifth U. S. Regulars, Lieutenant Weir. There were four of them'); July 3 fire discipline ('Our men were directed to withhold their fire' until 'about half way from the Emmitsburg road to our position'); 'I changed front forward on my right company' (vs Stannard's 'on first company' - wording conflict carried); 'We had fired about 10 rounds per man'; Doubleday ordering the regiments on his right to cease firing as the 13th passed their front; Hancock's wound then 'General Stannard was also wounded soon after'; casualties 'as near as I can now ascertain... 8 killed, 89 wounded and 26 missing' (vs the p. 174 return's 123 aggregate). Possible missing sentence(s) at the p. 352/353 transcription join; signature block not visible - recorded.

clockProfile.assessment: No statement about a chain anchor event (his only clocks are the July 1 arrival ~5 p.m. and a July 2 ~2 p.m. battery-support order). Kind-default envelope; profile exists to mark the report as nominal-clock class only.

sources.title: Vermont in the Civil War, vol. 2 (Gettysburg chapters)
sources.author: George Grenville Benedict
sources.editionNote: Participant-author: Benedict was Stannard's aide-de-camp at Gettysburg (named in Stannard's report p. 350). Accessed 2026-07-10 via the archive.org search-inside API (full djvu.txt too large to fetch whole); page-pinned snippets: p. 470 (arrival 'nearly sunset... across Willoughby Run'), p. 483 ('nearly 2,000 muskets'), p. 484 (Weir's battery rescue), pp. 495-497 (flank-attack geometry in rods), p. 501 (Wilcox/Perry identification), p. 502 (second attack at 'thirty or forty rods'), p. 503 (2nd Florida colors), p. 505 (Stannard's wound: shrapnel ball 'three inches into the muscles on the inside of the thigh'; refused to leave the field), p. 515 (mortally wounded lieutenants Sinnott/Hamilton/Lawton), pp. 518-521 (departure and muster-out dates). 25-year retrospective compiled from records; partisan for the brigade in the credit dispute (pp. 510-513, not page-verified this pass). No clock statements beyond 'nearly sunset' - no clockProfile assigned.

sources.title: Maj. B. F. Eshleman's report (Washington Artillery Battalion), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS transcription)
sources.author: Benjamin F. Eshleman
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. NPS transcription carries visible errors (quoted as printed, corrections bracketed in the dossiers: 'your ordered me'=you, 'Campaign Miller'=Captain, 'Fist Company'=First, 'Norcon'=Norcom, 'axe'=axle). Dated Aug. 11, 1863, addressed 'Colonel J. B. WALTON, Chief of Artillery, First Corps'. Key verbatim: midnight orders from Alexander, in position before daylight; per-company gun layout ~100 yards left of the peach orchard; 'Between 1 and 2 p. m. [you] ordered me to give the signal for opening along the entire line. Two guns in quick succession were fired from Captain Miller's battery'; 'About thirty minutes after the signal guns had been fired, our infantry moved forward over the plateau in our front' (AMBIGUOUS - charge vs infantry moving up; conflict-flagged in the dossier); Miller advanced '400 or 500 yards to the front' after the cannonade; the captured Union 3-inch rifle served by Richardson's company until disabled; casualties 'Wounded, 3 officers. Killed, 3; wounded, 23, and missing, 16... 37 horses killed and disabled; 3 guns disabled; 1 limber blown up.' OR print itself NOT reachable this pass (ehistory 403, Wayback blocked) - print check carried.

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement, itself an interval: signal order 'Between 1 and 2 p. m.' brackets the chain's 13:07; offset 0 adopted with the envelope spanning the stated interval's edges against the chain. His '[a]bout thirty minutes after the signal guns... our infantry moved forward' would put an infantry advance ~13:40 - it rides the ED-24 tension (2) short-cannonade side IF read as the charge, and is conflict-recorded, never anchor-defining (rule 4).

sources.title: Col. E. P. Alexander's report (battalion Reserve Artillery, First Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS transcription)
sources.author: Edward Porter Alexander
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Dated Aug. 3, 1863. Key verbatim: arrival 'at 9 a. m. on July 2'; July 2 'about 4 p. m. I placed four batteries... the whole commanded by Major Frank Huger' against the Peach Orchard ~500 yards off, 'a spirited engagement of a half hour', then occupied 'the enemy's original position'; July 3 'at dawn I again placed the whole battalion in position'; 'During the afternoon, the batteries all maintained their respective positions... driving off the enemy's sharpshooters with canister' (CONFLICTS with his SHSP 1877 'limber up and follow Pickett' - both carried); casualties 'killed, 19; wounded, 114; missing, 6; total, 139 men... Horses killed and disabled in action, 116.' No rounds-expended figures (verified negative). NPS transcription; OR print unreached this pass.

clockProfile.assessment: His report states no July 3 afternoon clock bearing on a chain anchor (the July 2 'about 4 p. m.' belongs to the ED-28 chain). Kind-default envelope; his 1877/1907 retrospectives carry the watch statements (see alexander-shsp4-1877, alexander-1907).

sources.title: Letter from General E. P. Alexander, late Chief of Artillery First Corps, A.N.V. (SHSP vol. 4 no. 3, Sept. 1877)
sources.author: Edward Porter Alexander
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Single-issue scan (the full-volume djvu.txt truncates; the sim_ issue worked). 14-year retrospective - closer to the event than the 1907 memoir. Key verbatim: 'about 75 guns which I then had in line'; 'It was 1 P. M. by my watch when the signal guns were fired'; the Longstreet-Alexander pre-cannonade note exchange; first note to Pickett timed 1:30 P.M. here vs 1:25 in the 1907 memoir (internal variant, flagged); 'If you are coming at all you must come immediately... at least 18 guns are still firing from the Cemetery itself'; 'For God's sake come quick; the 18 guns are gone'; ammunition: ordnance train 'about 100 rounds per gun additional' doubted, 'not over 20 rounds to the gun were left'; at the advance 'I galloped along my line of guns, ordering those that had over 20 rounds left to limber up and follow Pickett'. OCR flag: 'Hooker's corps at the Peach Orchard' is suspect OCR (Sickles's III Corps was the enemy there).

clockProfile.assessment: Same watch basis as alexander-1907 (ED-25 profile 3): '1 P. M. by my watch' at the 13:07 signal -> +7. His note times here (1:30) vs the memoir (1:25) differ by 5 min - inside the envelope; the profile is shared. 14-year vs 44-year distance argues slightly higher confidence than the memoir, capped by the same retrospection class.

sources.title: Letter of Col. J. B. Walton on the signal guns (SHSP vol. 5, pp. 47-53)
sources.author: James B. Walton
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Perseus page-by-page (pp. 47-53). Walton's defense of his command status in the Walton-Alexander controversy - parti pris on the command question, first-class on the order chain. Key verbatim: 'I had been for more than a year before, was during the battle and after the battle of Gettysburg, Chief of Artillery of the First Army Corps' (p. 47); Longstreet's order text 'Let the batteries open. Order great care and precision in firing...' (p. 50; Helen Longstreet's 1904 book prints the same note with slightly different wording - carried on longstreet-hightide-1904); 'fired the signal guns, as agreed with General Longstreet, to commence the bombardment' (pp. 50-51); reproduces Sorrel's July 1 '5:30 P. M.' march order to the battalions (p. 49) and Adjutant Owen's night-march account.

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement at fetch-model paraphrase grade (received Longstreet's note 'at approximately 1:00 P.M.') -> +7 vs the 13:07 signal, envelope widened for the paraphrase risk. 14-year retrospective in a command polemic; his sequence facts (order chain) are the evidentiary content, not his clock.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. W. N. Pendleton's report (Chief of Artillery, ANV), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 2 (NPS transcription)
sources.author: William N. Pendleton
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Key verbatim: the First Corps July 3 gun line order 'Henry's battalion held about its original position on the flank. Alexander's was next, in front of the peach orchard. Then came the Washington Artillery Battalion, under Major Eshleman, and Dearing's battalion on his left, these two having arrived since dusk of the day before... making nearly sixty guns for that wing'; opening 'At length, about 1 p. m., on the concerted signal, our guns in position, nearly one hundred and fifty, opened fire along the entire line'.

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement: 'about 1 p. m.' at the signal -> +7 vs 13:07. Report-nominal class (rule 4); envelope kind-default around the derived offset.

sources.title: In Camp and Battle with the Washington Artillery of New Orleans (and the United Service Magazine Aug. 1885 account)
sources.author: William Miller Owen
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. ACCESS FAILED for the Gettysburg chapter verbatim: every full-text route truncated before ch. XI (archive.org djvu.txt truncation; /download 403; B&L vol. 3 p. 362 footnote reproduction 403 at ehistory). The famous signal passage (courier at 1:30 P.M.; 'the report of the first gun rang out upon the still summer air'; the second gun) is carried at SNIPPET grade only and is NOT quotable as verified. What IS verified: Owen's night-march account as quoted inside Walton's SHSP 5 letter (walton-shsp5-1877, Perseus p. 49). Battalion adjutant; 22-year retrospective compiled from his war diary. Highest-value future fetch for the CA-J3A-1 executor detail (which gun, the delay).

sources.title: Lee and Longstreet at High Tide (Helen D. Longstreet), Longstreet's account of the 1:30 order to Walton
sources.author: Helen Dortch Longstreet (quoting James Longstreet)
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Gutenberg full text. Longstreet quoted: 'I wrote this note to Colonel Walton at exactly 1.30 P.M.: "Let the batteries open. Order great precision in firing..."' - a retrospective 13:30 claim for the order that his own 1863 report places 'About 2 p.m.' and the chain's signal evidence places ~13:07 (order necessarily earlier). Three mutually inconsistent Longstreet-side clocks for one event - carried as the order-chain's conflict record, never anchor-defining.

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement: the order to Walton 'at exactly 1.30 P.M.' must precede the 13:07 signal, so the stated clock runs at least ~23 min late against the chain -> -23 with a kind-widened envelope. 40-year retrospective contradicting his own report-nominal 'about 2 p.m.' (or-27-2-longstreet); the disagreement is the point.

sources.title: Addressing Gettysburg: Confederate order of battle (strengths/armament reproduction)
sources.author: Addressing Gettysburg
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Busey & Martin-type engaged strengths and armament as reproduced (the book itself remains unreachable - the standing busey-martin-2005 caveat applies; SECONDARY HOP flagged wherever cited). Used for: Eshleman's battalion 338 men/10 guns (1st Co. 77 men/1 Napoleon; 2nd 80/2 Napoleons+1 howitzer; 3rd 92/3 Napoleons; 4th 80/2 Napoleons+1 howitzer); Alexander's battalion 576 men/24 guns with per-battery figures (Moody 135, Woolfolk 103, Taylor 90, Parker 90, Jordan 78, Gilbert 71). Ashland armament discrepancy vs the park tablet (10-pdr vs 20-pdr Parrotts) recorded in the dossier. PASS-4 ADDITION (2026-07-11, the Kemper/Armistead morning-report sweep): the reproduction's Pickett-division engaged figures read - Garnett 1,459 (8Va 193, 18Va 312, 19Va 328, 28Va 333, 56Va 289), Kemper 1,634 (1Va 209, 3Va 322, 7Va 335, 11Va 359, 24Va 395), Armistead 2,055 (9Va 318, 14Va 422, 38Va 400, 53Va 435, 57Va 476), division 5,578. B&M-type hop flagged on every use (ED-38 pattern); no primary morning report for Kemper or Armistead exists in any OR page fetched across passes 1-4 (consistent with the Lee-Pickett suppression aftermath, or-27-3-lee-pickett) - the sweep is CLOSED as verified-negative for primaries.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Henry J. Hunt's report (Chief of Artillery, AoP), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 228-243 (encyc.org full-text reproduction)
sources.author: Henry J. Hunt
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Full report text read at encyc.org (ehistory's serial 043 has a page gap over pp. ~228-244 - 'Page not found' - so the OR print pagination is via the civilwarcycling index). Dated Sept. 27, 1863. Key verbatim: the 10 a.m. July 3 instruction ('to fire deliberately, and to husband their ammunition as much as possible'); 'the enemy opened, at about 1 p. m.... from one hundred to one hundred and twenty' guns vs 'more than eighty to reply effectively'; 'About 2.30 p. m., finding our ammunition running low... I directed that the fire should be gradually stopped, which was done, and the enemy soon slackened his fire also'; 'About 3 p. m., and soon after the enemy's fire had ceased, he formed a column of attack'; II Corps batteries 'having nearly exhausted their supply of ammunition, except canister'; Rorty's/Arnold's/Cushing's batteries withdrawn 'toward the close'; army expenditure '32,781 rounds, averaging over 100 rounds per gun' of 270 carried; the army artillery casualty table (total 320 guns; 7+98 killed, 33+532 wounded, 67 missing, 881 horses) and the July 3 officer-loss list (Rorty, Cushing killed; Woodruff, Milne mortally wounded).

clockProfile.assessment: Three anchor statements: opening 'about 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07), cease order 'About 2.30 p. m.' (0 vs the adopted ~14:30 slackening HE caused), assault forming 'About 3 p. m.' (+5 vs the 15:05 step-off frame). Median +5, remarkably consistent - but the CA-J3A-4 comparison is partially circular (his order defines the event), so kind stays report-nominal and rule 4 applies.

sources.title: The Third Day at Gettysburg (Battles & Leaders vol. 3, pp. 369-385)
sources.author: Henry J. Hunt
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. TWO reproductions used, and the difference matters: the historycentral text is ABRIDGED (it omits the cease-fire ride); the full text was read from the archive.org B&L vol. 3 raw OCR (https://ia800502.us.archive.org/8/items/battlesleadersof03john/battlesleadersof03john_djvu.txt, pp. 374-375). Key verbatim: 'Between 10 and 11 A.M.... I crossed over to Cemetery Ridge'; the withhold order 'to withhold their fire for fifteen or twenty minutes after the cannonade commenced... I had just given these orders to the last battery on Little Round Top, when the signal-gun was fired'; the cease-fire ride (Meade sought, Howard concurring, 'I rode back along the ridge, ordering the fire to cease. This was followed by a cessation of that of the enemy, under the mistaken impression that he had silenced our guns'); Meade's aides independently carrying cease-firing orders (Bingham); the dozen exploded caissons on the Taneytown pike; the Hazard-sector indictment ('They had unfortunately exhausted their long range projectiles during the cannonade, under the orders of their corps commander' - Hancock unnamed); gun counts (77 on the II Corps mile; McGilvery 'forty-one guns'; II Corps batteries reduced to four guns each). 24-year retrospective, parti pris in the Hunt-Hancock dispute - flagged.

clockProfile.assessment: His B&L narrative hangs on the signal gun firing as he finished the Little Round Top order (~13:07 chain) with his ride starting 'between 10 and 11'; no independent clock reading beyond the OR report's. Offset inherited from the OR profile's +5~+7 with a retrospection-widened envelope.

sources.title: Maj. Thomas W. Osborn's report (Artillery Brigade, XI Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 747-751 (No. 264)
sources.author: Thomas W. Osborn
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Read page-by-page at live ehistory (pp. 747-751; ehistory 403s WebFetch but loaded in the browser pane - access route recorded; OCR slips reproduced: 'Artilelry', 'shoved', 'charage', 'whetever'). Dated July 29, 1863, to Hunt. Key verbatim: July 1 roster 'Total, 26 guns' (Wilkeson mw July 1, 'the command of the battery now devolved upon Lieutenant Bancroft'); July 2 morning 52 guns on the hill with the six Reserve batteries; the hill layout (Dilger right resting on the Baltimore road... Taft and Huntington perpendicular across the road); July 3 'About 2 p. m. they opened along our whole front with an unbroken line of artillery'; HIS OWN cease order with its contemporary motive: 'but as no satisfactory results were obtained, I ordered all our guns to cease firing, and the men to lie down to await developments. At the same time the artillery of our entire front ceased firing, and a few moments later the infantry of the enemy broke over the crest'; the repulse fire ('the havoc produced upon their ranks was truly surprising'); rounds expended: 'I am unable to give any definite estimate' (verified negative - the circulating ~1,600 figure is NOT in his OR); materiel table (98 horses; guns disabled/dismounted per battery). The postwar ruse framing (Weekly Times 1879) vs this report's no-satisfactory-results motive is a first-class conflict, carried in the dossier.

clockProfile.assessment: One anchor statement: opening 'About 2 p. m.' -> raw -53 vs the 13:07 chain. Joins the ~14:00 nominal-opening cluster (Longstreet's report, Smyth's report, Stannard's report) - four reports across both armies now share the ~2 p.m. opening, strengthening the 'camp clock' cluster note on or-27-2-longstreet. Rule 4: never anchor-defining.

sources.title: Osborn, 'The Artillery at Gettysburg' (Philadelphia Weekly Times, May 31, 1879; repr. Crumb ed., The Eleventh Corps Artillery at Gettysburg)
sources.author: Thomas W. Osborn
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. NOT directly accessible - carried AS-QUOTED in the GNMP Seminar 4 essay (URL above, read in full) and Seminar 16 essay 4 PDF (Barnett, fn 25). Verified-as-quoted verbatim: the hill 'the best target for artillery practice the enemy had during the war'; 'the shells must have reached us at the rate of one hundred and upwards a minute'; Meade on the hill mid-cannonade ('He said the result of the fight depended on our holding the hill'); the Howard/Wainwright/Osborn command split on the hill. His cease-to-lure-the-assault conversation with Hunt (the 'ruse') could NOT be verbatim-verified through any reachable channel (verified absence in the reachable reproductions) - it rests on secondary paraphrase plus Hunt's B&L parallel (Howard concurrence). 16-year retrospective; the Crumb reprint is print-only.

sources.title: Capt. John G. Hazard's report (Artillery Brigade, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1, pp. 477-481 (No. 132)
sources.author: John G. Hazard
sources.editionNote: Accessed 2026-07-10. Read page-by-page at live ehistory (pp. 477-478, 480-481; p. 479 is a map page) and cross-checked verbatim-identical against the Iron Brigader reproduction (https://ironbrigader.com/2021/06/26/captain-john-g-hazards-report-on-the-union-2nd-corps-artillery-brigade-at-gettysburg/, first verified 2026-06-13). Dated Aug. 1, 1863. Key verbatim: the July 2 line ('On the right, in a grove, Light Company I; 150 yards to the left, Battery A, First Rhode Island... Battery A, Fourth U. S....'); July 2 losses (Brown's B: 1 k, 7 w, 2 m, 24 horses killed, two guns to the rear, Brown 'severely wounded in the neck by a musket-shot'; Rorty's 1 k, 8 w, 13 horses); July 3 ~8 a.m. exchange ('exploding three limbers of Battery A, Fourth U. S.'; Woodruff's battery 'during the forenoon had eight separate engagements'); the cannonade: 'At 1 p. m. the artillery of the enemy opened along the whole line, and for an hour and a quarter we were subjected to a very warm artillery fire. The batteries did not at first reply, till the fire of the enemy becoming too terrible, they returned it till all their ammunition, excepting canister, had been expended'; the assault ('half the valley had been passed over by them before the guns dared expend a round of the precious ammunition remaining'; canister 'with terrible effect'); withdrawals and the battery consolidation to three; officer fates (Rorty k after one day in command; Cushing 'mortally wounded by a musket-shot' k; Woodruff mw d. July 4 'at his own request, was buried on the field'; Milne 'musket-shot through the lungs' d. July 10; Sheldon w; 'Every officer in this battery [A/4th US] was either killed or wounded'); Fuger's field promotion recommendation; brigade loss 'one-third'.

clockProfile.assessment: Two anchor statements: opening 'At 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07) and duration 'an hour and a quarter' of heavy fire -> slackening ~14:15 nominal, inside CA-J3A-4's 14:00-15:00 envelope. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. William Harrow's report (2nd Division + 1st Brigade, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 419-421
sources.author: William Harrow
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated July 16, 1863; written as division report (Harrow succeeded Gibbon July 3). Keys: 'At 1 p. m. the enemy opened a fierce cannonade upon the line from a hundred or more guns, which was continued until nearly 3 p. m.'; 'the enemy moved from the woods, 1,000 yards in front'; 'the crest of the hill occupied by the right of Colonel Hall and the left of General Webb seemed to be the point to which their main attack was directed'; 'the Third and First Brigades, of this division, inclined to the right, engaging the enemy as they moved'; 'the contest raged with almost unparalleled ferocity for nearly an hour'; division 'took into action 3,773 men, and lost 1,657'; 1st Brigade two-day loss 722 (148 k / 574 w); officer pins Col. Ward (15th MA) and Col. Huston (82nd NY) mw July 2, Colvill/Adams/Downie (1st MN) shot down July 3.

clockProfile.assessment: Opening 'At 1 p. m.' vs chain 13:07 (+7); cannonade 'continued until nearly 3 p. m.' vs CA-J3A-5 15:00 (consistent, +0..+7). Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Col. Thomas A. Smyth's report (2nd Brigade, 3rd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 464-466
sources.author: Thomas A. Smyth
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route; OCR renders the name 'Smith'). Keys: July 1 'order to march at 6.30 a. m.' from Uniontown; July 2 'At 4 a. m. ... placed in position on the hills overlooking the town', 'At 8 a. m. the First Delaware Volunteers were sent out as skirmishers, and the One hundred and eighth New York Volunteers were assigned to the support of Woodruff's battery', 'At 2 p. m. the enemy opened upon us with a severe fire of artillery, accompanied by an advance of infantry' (the Bliss farm axis); July 3 'Artillery firing from both sides began at 4 a. m.', 'Skirmishing ... until 10.30 a. m.', 'At 2 p. m. a most terrific cannonading was opened upon our front' 'continued without intermission until 5 p. m.', 'As the fire of the enemy's batteries slackened, their infantry moved upon our position in three lines'; 12th NJ held fire 'until they were within 20 yards'; prisoners 'estimated at from 1,200 to 1,500', 9 battle-flags; 108th NY casualties 'about half of the regiment in action'; Smyth wounded during the cannonading, 'surrendered the command to Lieutenant Colonel Francis E. Pierce'; Lt. William Smith (1st DE) killed 'with one of the enemy's captured flags in his hand'; brigade casualty recapitulation totaling 352.

clockProfile.assessment: Joins the ~14:00 nominal cluster (Longstreet, Osborn, Stannard): cannonade 'At 2 p. m.' vs chain 13:07 -> -53. His 'until 5 p. m.' end is 2h late against CA-J3A-5 and appears to fold the assault and after-fire into the cannonade window; envelope kept one-sided. Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Lt. Col. James M. Bull's report (3rd Brigade [Willard's/Sherrill's], 3rd Division, II Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 472-474
sources.author: James M. Bull
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: July 2 'first took its position, by direction of the division general, by battalion in mass near a barn a little south of ... Cemetery Hill'; 39th NY (Hildebrandt) skirmish, 'lost 26 enlisted men killed and wounded, and 2 commissioned officers wounded'; 'At about 3 p. m. ... the brigade moved from its position by the left flank about a quarter of a mile toward the left of the line' (July 2, then the Plum Run charge under Willard, 'pursuant to the order of Colonel Willard, given just before his death'); July 3 'About 1 p. m. the enemy opened from his batteries ... a terrific cannonade ensued, which continued about two hours'; 'The enemy, advancing in four lines across the flat, were subjected to a murderous fire of musketry and artillery, and were driven back in confusion, after an engagement of about an hour'; regiments 'so placed as supports to the artillery' on 'the slope of the hill occupied by us'; 'the brigade was commanded by Colonel Sherrill until his death, about 4 p. m., when the command devolved upon Lieutenant Colonel James M. Bull'; brigade loss 'severe ... the precise number of which I am unable to report' (attested-unreported at brigade grain).

clockProfile.assessment: Opening 'About 1 p. m.' (+7 vs 13:07); 'continued about two hours' spans to ~15:00 nominal, consistent with CA-J3A-4/5. His 'Sherrill ... death, about 4 p. m.' sits ~30-40 min after the repulse window on the corrected clock - either a late-window casualty or clock drift; envelope widened, conflict carried on the dossier. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Capt. Andrew Cowan's report (1st New York Independent Battery), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 689-691
sources.author: Andrew Cowan
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated Aug. 15, 1863, Warrenton Va. Keys: left Manchester 21:00 July 1, arrived near the field '10 p. m. the 2d' (VI Corps march); July 3 'reported to General Newton ... about 10 a. m.'; heavy engagement 'about noon' (his opening clock - the corpus's second early pole after McGilvery's 12:30); parked 'less than 100 yards distant' behind the front line; moved at the crisis to the 'crest farther to my right, with General Webb's brigade' (the Brown-slot geometry); 'I commenced firing canister at 200 yards ... My last charge literally swept the enemy from my front, being fired at less than 20 yards'; counter-battery: 'concentrated my fire on a single battery, and exploded four of its limbers in rapid succession'; casualties '4 privates killed instantly, and 1 soon after died of wounds; 4 enlisted men and 2 officers wounded', 14 horses, 8 wheels disabled. No rounds-expended figure in the report (negative).

clockProfile.assessment: Single anchor statement: cannonade opened 'about noon' vs chain 13:07 -> +67 (his stated clock runs very early, the McGilvery pattern). One-source, one-statement; envelope reaches 0 because 'about noon' may loosely cover the pre-cannonade exchanges. Rule 4: display-only, never corrects.

sources.title: Lt. Gulian V. Weir's report (Battery C, 5th US), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 879-880
sources.author: Gulian V. Weir
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: July 2 'About 4 o'clock was ordered by Major-General Hancock to take up a position' forward; 'I opened with solid shot and spherical case'; 'Soon it was reported to me that we were out of canister' (the gun-loss fight; three guns temporarily lost, recaptured by the 13th VT per the battery tablet); July 3 'the enemy opened fire along the whole line, and it became so hot that we fell back'; 'I was conducted to General Webb's position, and came into battery under a heavy musketry fire'; ROUNDS EXPENDED VERBATIM: '50 rounds of solid shot, 160 rounds of spherical case, and 70 rounds of canister; total, 280 rounds'; Lt. Baldwin wounded by shell fragment, one caisson lost.

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon anchor clock stated (his only clock is July 2 'about 4 o'clock'). Profile recorded as unanchored nominal; timing of his July 3 movement is carried tier-D against the crisis sequence.

sources.title: Capt. Robert H. Fitzhugh's report (4th Volunteer Brigade + Battery K 1st NY), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 895-897
sources.author: Robert H. Fitzhugh
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: ordered by Tyler to post K/1st NY and A/1st NJ on the Baltimore turnpike near Slocum's line (July 2-3); 'At 1 p. m.' Friday July 3, by Hunt's direction, both batteries deployed 'near the stone fence in front of General Webb's division of the Second Corps'; enemy infantry reached the stone fence 'approximately 75 yards' from his position; Parsons delivered 'about 40 rounds of shrapnel into the flank of the rebel infantry charging the Second corps'; attack collapsed 'within thirty to forty-five minutes'; second assault (Wilcox/Lang axis) broken mid-field by 'an enfilading fire'; ROUNDS (K + attached 11th NY): '57 percussion shells, 15 shrapnel rounds, 17 time shells (89 total)'; casualties K: 7 wounded, 5 horses; A/1st NJ: 2 killed.

clockProfile.assessment: His 'At 1 p. m.' for the move to Webb's front is irreconcilable with the crisis sequence (the charge reached the wall ~15:20-25; Stone Sentinels' narrative has the batteries 'ordered into line at the gallop' at the height of the charge, and Parsons's own report says the order came 'about 3 p. m.'). Read as either the initial ORDER clock at the cannonade's opening (+7) or a plain misstatement; the huge negative envelope records the conflict. Rule 4: never moves anchors; the dossier carries the conflict. (Envelope floor clipped to the schema's -120 min bound; the raw conflict is larger and lives in the editionNote.)

sources.title: Lt. Augustin N. Parsons's report (Battery A, 1st New Jersey), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 899-900
sources.author: Augustin N. Parsons
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated July 17, 1863. Keys: 'On the 3rd instant, about 3 p. m., I received orders from General Hunt to move the battery'; placed 'about one-fourth of a mile south of Gettysburg Cemetery and near the Second Division, Second Corps'; 'At this time the enemy's infantry were advancing very rapidly. I at once opened fire upon them with case'; Fitzhugh's battery took position immediately on his right; then counter-battery: 'fired about 120 rounds with good effect' (shot) at one battery and 'used 80 rounds' (shell) at another before Hunt ordered cease; casualties 2 killed, 7 wounded; 3 horses killed + 2 mortally.

clockProfile.assessment: His 'about 3 p. m.' order-to-move with infantry 'advancing very rapidly' on arrival maps to ~15:10-15:20 canonical (between step-off and the wall) -> +10..+20. Consistent with the crisis sequence; rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Lt. William Wheeler's report (13th New York Independent Battery), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 752-753
sources.author: William Wheeler
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Dated July 29, 1863, Warrenton Junction. Keys: July 1 'orders to move forward at double-quick' through the town to Dilger's right, two caisson bodies broke down, 'very heavy fire ... both in front and upon the right flank'; July 3 'At approximately 4 p.m. ... orders to go assist the Second Corps, upon which a very heavy attack was being made'; Hancock assigned the position; found the enemy 'not more than 400 yards off, marching in heavy column by a flank'; 'enfilade their column with canister, which threw them into great disorder, and brought them to a halt three times'; ROUNDS EXPENDED: 850 total (whole battle); casualties 4 severely + 6 slightly wounded, 3 missing, 12 horses killed.

clockProfile.assessment: His 'about 4 p. m.' for the II Corps crisis move sits ~35-45 min after the canonical crisis (wall fighting 15:20-15:30); either his clock runs late or the battery arrived at the tail of the repulse (his canister enfilade on a column halting three times reads mid-fight, favoring clock error). Rule 4: display-only.

sources.title: Capt. James H. Cooper's report (Battery B, 1st Pennsylvania Light), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 364-365
sources.author: James H. Cooper
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route). Keys: July 1 arrived ~noon, posted by Wainwright left of 3rd Div I Corps, 'fired about twenty-five shots at a battery in front', relocated near the Seminary, case/shell 'until canister range was obtained'; July 2 on Cemetery Hill crest 'fronting to the northeast', 16:00 duel at 1,400-2,000 yards, 'the enemy's guns were silenced in about two and a half hours' firing', relieved by Ricketts ~19:00, to the Artillery Reserve to refit; July 2 casualties 2 k (McCleary, Hoagland), 6 w, ~500 rounds; July 3 'Around 3 p. m., the battery moved to Second Corps front' engaging a damaged enemy battery then 'case shot until they reached canister range, when a few charges were fired into them, completely routing them'; July 3: 1 slightly wounded, 150 rounds. ROUNDS EXPENDED, PER DAY: ~400 (Jul 1, implied by total) / ~500 (Jul 2) / 150 (Jul 3); 'Total: 1,050 rounds fired across three days' (monument concurs at 1050).

clockProfile.assessment: July 3 'about 3 p. m.' move to the Second Corps front during the cannonade's last phase / opening of the assault (~15:00-15:10 canonical) -> ~+7; the War Dept tablet independently adjudicates the move 'at 3 P. M. ... during a heavy cannonade'. Rule 4 applies.

sources.title: Col. Charles S. Wainwright's report (Artillery Brigade, I Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 354-359
sources.author: Charles S. Wainwright
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route; pp. 355, 357-359 read; p. 356 not fetched this pass). Keys: July 1 Seminary Ridge fight and the retirement under Wadsworth's 'peremptory order'; East Cemetery Hill layout verbatim: 'Four guns of Battery B, Fourth U. S. Artillery, across the road so as to command the approaches from the town ... four guns of Battery I, First New York Artillery ... on the left; next Cooper's battery and then Reynolds'' (13 three-inch guns on the north front); 'The Fifth Maine battery was posted to the right and some 50 yards in front of this line, on a small knoll'; July 1 loss 'amounting in all to 83 officers and men and about 80 horses'; July 2 evening: CSA battery '(four 20-pounders and six 10-pounder Parrotts) in a wheat-field ... at about 1,300 yards' silenced in ~90 min leaving 'twenty-eight dead horses'; the dusk assault on the hill: 'fifteen guns in their front and six on their left flank', cannoneers of Wiedrich's and Ricketts' repelling attackers 'with fence-rails and stones'; his judgment 'this attack was almost entirely repelled by the artillery'; July 3: 'fired occasional shots at bodies of the enemy's troops' in the morning and participated in the afternoon's general engagement (no brigade rounds total in the report; component battery reports carry the figures: Cooper 1,050, Breck L/1st NY 1,290 itemized, Stevens 5th ME etc.); 1st Sgt Mitchell commanding B/4th US after Lt. Davison wounded. PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 356-359 fetched 2026-07-11): the p. 356 gap is CLOSED - it is July 1 narrative (the Seminary-crest twelve-gun concentration, guns 'hardly 5 yards apart'); the report contains NO brigade strength or rounds roll-up anywhere (now attested-absent, upgraded from unfetched): the only aggregate is July 1 'loss of the batteries ... amounting in all to 83 officers and men and about 80 horses'. July 2: the 4 p.m. Benner's Hill duel at 1,300 yards ('the most accurate fire I have ever yet seen from their artillery', an hour and a half to silence); the dusk East Cemetery Hill infantry attack repelled 'almost entirely ... by the artillery'. July 3: 'no serious attack upon the position ... The batteries fired occasional shots ... during the morning, and joined in the general artillery engagement in the afternoon. The fire of the enemy's batteries was noticed to be much less accurate than on the previous day.'

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon anchor clock in the fetched pages; July 1-2 clocks internally consistent with the provisional chains. Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Brig. Gen. Robert O. Tyler's report (Artillery Reserve) with Lt. Gillett's ordnance annex, OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 871-879
sources.author: Robert O. Tyler
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (civilwar.com route; pp. 871-873, 878-879 read). Dated Aug. 30, 1863. Keys: June 29-30 Frederick -> Bruceville -> Taneytown; July 1 reported to Gibbon '10.30 p. m.' with eight batteries; July 2 parked 'about 1 1/2 miles from Gettysburg'; 'six batteries of thirty-four guns' sent to Sickles, 'under Major McGilvery ... doing terrible execution upon the successive columns attacking our left until about 6 p. m.'; B and M 1st CT Heavy ordered to Westminster (guns short two batteries); guns left on the field July 2 recovered after dark 'all but one gun' (Thompson's); Turnbull's battery July 3: '1 officer and 8 men killed, 14 men wounded, and 45 horses killed', four guns retaken; Ransom's regular brigade 'formed on hill crest near General Meade's headquarters'; 'No report has yet been received from Captain Ransom's ... or Captain Sterling's ... batteries'. GILLETT ORDNANCE ANNEX (2nd Lt. C. Gillett, ordnance officer, pp. 878-879) VERBATIM KEYS: ammunition ISSUED at Gettysburg '19,189' rounds across calibers; '4,694 rounds' remaining 'nearly all of which, though not in the best possible condition, could have been used'; 'Seventy wagons were unloaded, which were sent to the rear on the morning of the 3d'; caution that 'sending wagons loaded with ammunition to batteries in action often causes a loss of material'. This annex is the army-side ordnance-return class evidence behind Hunt's ~270 rounds/gun arithmetic. PASS-3 UPGRADE (pp. 874-878 fetched 2026-07-11): the report's own totals - '108 guns which were on the field'; 'seventy wagon-loads were issued on the night of the 2nd'; '10,090 rounds were issued to batteries outside of the reserve during the battle'; Fitzhugh and Parsons 'came up very opportunely to the support of the troops of Brigadier-General Webb' (ED-41 corroboration); Tyler's horse killed at the park as the cannonade opened ~12 o'clock (his clock). APPENDIX A (p. 876, July 4, 2 p.m.): 14 officers wounded, 216 privates k/w/m, 'Rounds of ammunition expended, about 11,653', 'Rounds of ammunition on hand 3,900' - the Reserve's own EXPENDITURE-side ledger, cleanly distinct from the issue-side figures (the ED-42 exemplar made explicit by the primary itself). Per-battery casualty table with officer pins at p. 878 (Ransom and Baldwin w; Livingston k; ...).

clockProfile.assessment: No July 3 afternoon anchor clocks in the fetched pages (his July 2 'until about 6 p. m.' is a July 2 statement). Unanchored nominal.

sources.title: Capt. A. P. Martin's report (Artillery Brigade, V Corps), OR Series I Vol. 27 Part 1 pp. 659-661
sources.author: Augustus P. Martin
sources.editionNote: Fetch-verified 2026-07-11 (p. 661 read; brigade-level only). Rittenhouse's D/5th US filed NO separate report (verified negative this pass: p. 661 references Battery D only as a position datum for other units, 'to the right of Battery D, Fifth U. S. Artillery'). Brigade casualties 'Killed, 9; wounded, 33, and missing, 2. Total, 44.'

sources.title: The Battle of Gettysburg as Seen from Little Round Top (MOLLUS DC Commandery war paper, read May 4, 1887)
sources.author: Benjamin F. Rittenhouse
sources.editionNote: ACCESS FAILED AGAIN 2026-07-11 (pass 3 alternate-route hunt): (1) the recorded gdg.org URL is a MISATTRIBUTION - mollus16.html serves 'THE NINETEENTH MAINE AT GETTYSBURG' by Capt. Substitute evidence found this pass: the RECEIVING-side primary - Peyton's report describes the LRT enfilade on Garnett's line ('one [battery] posted on the mountain, about 1 mile to our right ... sometimes as many as 10 men being killed and wounded by the bursting of a single shell'), and the Gettysburg Magazine 'Measuring Pickett's Charge' terrain study (gdg.org reproduction) adds that only the battery's north-facing Parrotts bore past the Codori farm. The paper remains the EC5 upgrade gate for the battery's own account; recommend a library/CDL acquisition item.
